title: Intro
text: Welcome to Science Radar, your weekly digest of the most surprising, frontier-pushing, and cross-disciplinary discoveries from across all of science. This week we've found papers that challenge long-held assumptions, open doors to entirely new research directions, and connect ideas across fields in ways nobody expected. Let's dive in.

title: This Week's Highlights
text: And now, a quick tour through this week's other noteworthy papers.

title: Precision cutaneous stimulation in freely moving mice
text: Precision cutaneous stimulation in freely moving mice — eLife. Somatosensation connects animals to their immediate environment, shaping critical behaviors essential for adaptation, learning, and survival. Probing the relationships between somatosensory inputs and behavior in mice presents substantial challenges, primarily due to the practical difficulties of de

title: Cell type-specific network analysis in Diversity Outbred mice identifies genes potentially responsible for human bone mineral density GWAS associations
text: Cell type-specific network analysis in Diversity Outbred mice identifies genes potentially responsible for human bone mineral density GWAS associations — eLife. Genome-wide association studies (GWASs) have identified many sources of genetic variation associated with bone mineral density (BMD), a clinical predictor of fracture risk and osteoporosis. Aside from the identification of causal genes, other difficult challenges to informing GWAS include characteri

title: How an all-female fish species defies evolutionary expectations
text:
How an all-female fish species defies evolutionary expectations — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 11 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00493-0

Asexual reproduction should be an evolutionary dead end. A study of asexual fish shows that a genetic ‘copy and paste’ helps to overcome the predicted costs

title: This fish shouldn’t exist — the weird genetics of clonal vertebrates
text:
This fish shouldn’t exist — the weird genetics of clonal vertebrates — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 11 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00798-0

A study reveals how the asexual Amazon molly defies evolutionary expectations — plus, evidence of what might be powering superluminous supernovae.

title: Using mosquitoes to vaccinate bats could curb the spread of deadly diseases
text:
Using mosquitoes to vaccinate bats could curb the spread of deadly diseases — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 11 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00795-3

But scientists say there are practical and ethical challenges to overcome before the strategy could be deployed in real-world settings.

text:

by Priyanka Panchal, Vishal Polara, Siddaraj U, Abdullah Baz, Shobhit K. Patel

title: Cardenolide toxin diversity impacts monarch butterfly growth and sequestration
text: Cardenolide toxin diversity impacts monarch butterfly growth and sequestration — eLife. In coevolutionary interactions, host plants accrue novel chemical defenses that specialist herbivores counter by detoxification and sometimes sequestration. title: Self-association enhances early attentional selection through automatic prioritization of socially salient signals
text: Self-association enhances early attentional selection through automatic prioritization of socially salient signals — eLife. Efficiently processing self-related information is critical for cognition, yet the earliest mechanisms enabling this self-prioritization in humans remain unclear. By combining a temporal order judgement task with computational modeling based on the Theory of Visual Attention (TVA), we show how mere,

title: Cardiorespiratory fitness attenuates the association between psychosocial stress and cardiometabolic risk – Results from the SCAPIS population

title: Effect of growth hormone adjuvant treatment on oocyte retrieval in patients with poor ovarian response and no viable embryos in previous in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles
text:
Effect of growth hormone adjuvant treatment on oocyte retrieval in patients with poor ovarian response and no viable embryos in previous in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles — PeerJ. Background
               This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy of growth hormone (GH) as an adjuvant treatment for enhancing embryo quantity in patients with poor ovarian response (POR) who previously had no viable embryos in in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles.

title: Effect of the interpregnancy interval after early pregnancy loss on pregnancy outcomes after subsequent embryo transfer: a retrospective cohort study
text:
Effect of the interpregnancy interval after early pregnancy loss on pregnancy outcomes after subsequent embryo transfer: a retrospective cohort study — PeerJ. Background
               After early pregnancy loss (EPL), couples often seek counselling on how long to wait before attempting to conceive again. However, the optimal interpregnancy interval (IPI) between EPL and the next pregnancy is controversial. Additionally, studies on the impact of the IPI f

title: Transcriptome analysis reveals heat stress-responsive genes in octoploid strawberry seedlings and expression pattern analysis of FaHSF gene family under heat stress
text: Transcriptome analysis reveals heat stress-responsive genes in octoploid strawberry seedlings and expression pattern analysis of FaHSF gene family under heat stress — PeerJ. Extreme high temperatures severely affect the cultivation of octoploid strawberry seedlings. However, the molecular mechanisms of heat stress response in strawberry seedlings remain unclear. The results demonstrated that when strawberry seedlings were subjected to 40 °C treatment for 6 hours, the pr

title: Development and validation of early-stage and progression prediction models for chronic kidney disease: a retrospective study
text:
Development and validation of early-stage and progression prediction models for chronic kidney disease: a retrospective study — PeerJ. Objectives
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) poses a significant public health burden. This study aimed to evaluate the associations between clinical laboratory indices and CKD and to develop prediction and  prognostic models for CKD risk assessment and disease progression.

title: Outro
text: That's Science Radar for this week. Every one of these papers is a reminder that the most interesting science often lives at the edges — where fields collide, where assumptions break, and where the next big question is just starting to take shape. Until next week, keep questioning everything.
